questions.stem: In cell division, a parent cell divides to produce:
questions.explanation: A parent (mother) cell divides to produce two daughter cells.

questions.stem: Cytokinesis is the:

options.text: Splitting of the cytoplasm between daughter cells

questions.explanation: Cytokinesis splits the cytoplasm of a parent cell between the two daughter cells.

questions.scenario: An animal cell divides its cytoplasm.
questions.stem: How is this achieved?

options.text: A ring of actin and myosin pinches the membrane
Vesicles build a new cell wall

questions.explanation: In animal cells, a ring of contractile actin and myosin pinches the membrane to split the cytoplasm.

questions.scenario: A plant cell divides its cytoplasm.
questions.stem: How is this achieved?

options.text: Vesicles assemble new membrane and cell wall
A ring of actin and myosin pinches inward

questions.explanation: In plant cells, vesicles assemble sections of membrane and cell wall to split the cytoplasm.

questions.scenario: During oogenesis, cytoplasm is divided unequally.
questions.stem: This is an example of:
questions.explanation: Cytokinesis is usually equal, but oogenesis in humans and budding in yeast are examples of unequal cytokinesis.

questions.stem: During cytokinesis, each daughter cell must receive:
questions.explanation: Each daughter cell must receive at least one mitochondrion and any organelle that can only be made by dividing a pre-existing one.

questions.stem: Which process maintains the chromosome number and genome of cells?
questions.explanation: Mitosis maintains chromosome number and the genome; meiosis halves the chromosome number and generates diversity.

questions.stem: Why must nuclear division occur before cell division?

options.text: To avoid producing cells without a nucleus

questions.explanation: Nuclear division is needed before cell division to avoid producing anucleate (nucleus-less) cells.

questions.stem: After replication, each chromosome consists of:
questions.explanation: After replication, each chromosome consists of two chromatids held together until anaphase.

questions.stem: During both mitosis and meiosis, DNA is condensed by:
questions.explanation: Histones help condense DNA by supercoiling; microtubules and motors then move the chromosomes.

questions.stem: Chromosomes are moved during division by:
questions.explanation: Microtubules and microtubule motors move the chromosomes during mitosis and meiosis.

questions.stem: Mitosis produces:
questions.explanation: Mitosis produces two genetically identical daughter cells.

questions.stem: Which is a correct phase of mitosis?
questions.explanation: The phases of mitosis are prophase, metaphase, anaphase and telophase.

questions.scenario: A student identifies mitosis phases in a micrograph.
questions.stem: During anaphase, the chromatids:

options.text: Separate and move to opposite poles

questions.explanation: In anaphase the sister chromatids separate and move to opposite poles of the cell.

questions.stem: Meiosis is described as a reduction division because it:

options.text: Keeps the chromosome number the same

questions.explanation: Meiosis is a reduction division: it produces four haploid nuclei from one diploid nucleus, halving the chromosome number.

questions.stem: A diploid cell has how many sets of chromosomes?
questions.explanation: Diploid cells have two sets of chromosomes; haploid cells (such as gametes) have one.

questions.stem: How many haploid nuclei does meiosis produce from one diploid nucleus?
questions.explanation: The two divisions of meiosis produce four haploid nuclei from one diploid nucleus.

questions.scenario: Down syndrome results from an error in meiosis.
questions.stem: This error is called:
questions.explanation: Down syndrome is an example of non-disjunction, where chromosomes fail to separate correctly in meiosis.

questions.stem: Which process during meiosis generates genetic diversity?

options.text: Random orientation of bivalents and crossing over

questions.explanation: Meiosis generates genetic diversity through random orientation of bivalents and crossing over.

questions.scenario: During meiosis, sections of homologous chromosomes are exchanged.
questions.stem: This process is called:
questions.explanation: Crossing over exchanges sections between homologous chromosomes, contributing to genetic diversity.

questions.stem: Which process is needed for producing gametes in a sexual life cycle?
questions.explanation: Meiosis halves the chromosome number to produce haploid gametes needed in a sexual life cycle.

questions.stem: During metaphase of mitosis, chromosomes:

options.text: Line up at the equator of the cell

questions.explanation: In metaphase the chromosomes line up at the cell's equator before the chromatids separate in anaphase.

questions.stem: DNA replication is a prerequisite for mitosis and meiosis because it:

options.text: Provides two chromatids per chromosome to distribute

questions.explanation: Replication produces two chromatids per chromosome so copies can be distributed to daughter cells or nuclei.

questions.scenario: In non-disjunction, chromosomes fail to separate.
questions.stem: The resulting gamete may have:

options.text: The exact normal chromosome number always

questions.explanation: Non-disjunction produces gametes with an abnormal chromosome number, as in Down syndrome.

questions.stem: New cells in living organisms are generated by:
questions.explanation: New cells are generated by cell division, in which a parent cell divides into daughter cells.

questions.stem: How many rounds of division occur in meiosis?
questions.explanation: Meiosis has two divisions (two rounds of segregation), producing four haploid nuclei.

questions.stem: The overall result of mitosis is important for:

options.text: Growth and tissue repair with identical cells

questions.explanation: Mitosis produces genetically identical cells, important for growth, tissue replacement and repair.

questions.scenario: Budding in yeast produces a small and a large cell.
questions.stem: This is an example of:
questions.explanation: Budding in yeast and oogenesis in humans are examples of unequal cytokinesis.

questions.stem: Condensation of chromosomes is a shared feature of:
questions.explanation: Condensation and movement of chromosomes are features shared by both mitosis and meiosis.

questions.scenario: Bivalents orient randomly at the equator during meiosis.
questions.stem: This random orientation:

options.text: Increases the variety of gametes produced

questions.explanation: Random orientation of bivalents produces many different combinations of chromosomes in gametes, increasing diversity.

questions.stem: Which correctly contrasts mitosis and meiosis?

options.text: Mitosis keeps chromosome number; meiosis halves it
Mitosis halves chromosome number; meiosis keeps it

questions.explanation: Mitosis maintains chromosome number and produces identical cells; meiosis halves it and generates variation.

questions.stem: The two chromatids of a chromosome are held together until:
questions.explanation: After replication each chromosome has two chromatids held together until anaphase, when they separate.

questions.stem: A haploid cell is one that has:
questions.explanation: Haploid cells have one set of chromosomes; gametes are haploid, formed by meiosis from diploid cells.

questions.stem: Cell proliferation for growth occurs in plants within:
questions.explanation: Plant meristems are sites of cell proliferation for growth; early-stage animal embryos are another example.

questions.scenario: Skin cells are constantly replaced and repaired.
questions.stem: This is an example of cell proliferation for:
questions.explanation: Skin illustrates cell proliferation during routine cell replacement and during wound healing.

questions.stem: Which is the correct order of the cell cycle stages?
questions.explanation: The cell cycle runs G1, S and G2 (interphase), then mitosis and cytokinesis.

questions.scenario: DNA is replicated during one stage of interphase.
questions.stem: Which stage is this?
questions.explanation: DNA is synthesized (replicated) during the S phase of interphase.

questions.stem: Interphase is described as a period that is:

options.text: Metabolically active, with growth and biosynthesis
A resting stage with no activity
When the cell divides its nucleus

questions.explanation: Interphase is metabolically active: the cell grows and carries out biosynthesis of proteins and DNA.

questions.scenario: During interphase the number of mitochondria increases.
questions.stem: This occurs by:

options.text: Growth and division of existing organelles

questions.explanation: Numbers of mitochondria and chloroplasts increase during interphase by growth and division of these organelles.

questions.stem: The cell cycle is controlled by proteins called:
questions.explanation: Cyclins control the cell cycle; a threshold level of a specific cyclin is required to pass each checkpoint.

questions.stem: How do cyclin concentrations behave during the cell cycle?

options.text: They increase and decrease at different points

questions.explanation: Different cyclins increase and decrease during the cycle; a threshold level is needed to pass each checkpoint.

questions.scenario: A mutation converts a proto-oncogene into an oncogene.
questions.stem: The likely consequence is:
questions.explanation: Mutations converting proto-oncogenes to oncogenes, or disabling tumour suppressor genes, cause uncontrolled cell division.

questions.stem: Mutations in which type of gene can remove the normal brakes on cell division?
questions.explanation: Mutations in tumour suppressor genes (and proto-oncogenes) can lead to uncontrolled cell division.

questions.scenario: A tumour does not spread to other tissues.
questions.stem: It is best described as:
questions.explanation: A benign tumour does not invade or spread; a malignant tumour can metastasize and cause cancer.

questions.scenario: Cancer cells break away from a primary tumour and form new tumours elsewhere.
questions.stem: This spreading is called:
questions.explanation: Metastasis is the spread of malignant cells to form secondary tumours, distinguishing malignant from benign tumours.

questions.scenario: A student observes cells to find the proportion undergoing mitosis.
questions.stem: This proportion is the:
questions.explanation: The mitotic index is the proportion of cells undergoing mitosis, determined by observing cell populations.

questions.stem: Which stages together make up interphase?
questions.explanation: Interphase consists of G1, S and G2, followed by mitosis and then cytokinesis.

questions.scenario: A specific cyclin does not reach its threshold level.
questions.stem: What happens at the checkpoint?

options.text: The cell cannot pass that checkpoint

questions.explanation: A threshold level of the specific cyclin is required to pass each checkpoint, so the cycle stops if it is not reached.

questions.stem: Cancer arises when mutations lead to:

options.text: Cells that cannot divide at all

questions.explanation: Mutations in genes controlling the cell cycle (proto-oncogenes and tumour suppressor genes) cause uncontrolled division.

questions.scenario: A tumour that has spread has formed a new growth in a distant organ.
questions.stem: The new growth is a:
questions.explanation: A secondary tumour forms when malignant cells metastasize from the primary tumour to a new location.
